learning_outcomes.description: Apply the kinetics of non-reversible reactions to determine orders of reaction, reaction rates, rate constants and relate simple reaction mechanisms to rate laws.

Represent selected reaction pathways using accepted organic chemistry conventions. Describe the shapes and bonding of organic molecules and use these to predict the reactivity of various functional groups and outcomes of selected reactions.
Apply concepts related to weak acids and bases to aqueous solutions and buffers.
Determine simple chemical structures by Mass Spectrometry (MS) and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy.
Apply crystal field theory to the bonding and visible spectroscopy of transition metal compounds.
Demonstrate practical competencies in a basic set of laboratory techniques.

assessments.due_date:
27/07/2026 - 30/10/2026

Assessment completed after the respective laboratory session
assessments.task_description:
In CHEM1200 in semester 2 there two ways to complete your laboratory component; you have the option of participating in the 5 traditional laboratory experiments OR in an undergradute reseach experience in the laboratory.

Option 1: 5 experiments

A laboratory induction and 5 experiments (each of 3 hours duration) will be completed on the 2nd Floor Chemistry Laboratory, Building 68. The date of commencement of laboratory classes as well as a timetable for the laboratory classes will be posted on the CHEM1200 Blackboard site.  Please note dates, times and experiments carefully. Each student will be issued with a Laboratory Manual.

Please note that you should read the laboratory notes, complete a pre-lab quiz, a flow chart and a ChemWatch table prior to coming to the laboratory. If you do not complete these items prior to coming to the laboratory, you will not be able to complete the laboratory session, and a rescheduling penalty (see below) will apply. Protective glasses must be worn in the laboratory. Appropriate footwear is also essential, as is a laboratory coat. If a student does not wear appropriate footwear, protective glasses and a lab coat they cannot gain entry to the lab, and a rescheduling penalty will be applied. 

Completion of the laboratory sessions is COMPULSORY. If you have a valid reason for absence (e.g. illness) you must provide appropriate documentary evidence (a medical certificate). In this case the practical will be rescheduled without penalty. If a student does not arrive on time at an allocated practical session (that is within 15 min of the allocated time) and does not provide appropriate documentation, the rescheduling of the practical session will result in a mark penalty (see below).

The laboratory component for the course is worth 25% of the total assessment for the course. The assessment of laboratory experiments is criteria based. Scores ranging from 1-25 are allocated for each experiment depending on how well students satisfy the list of criteria set down for the experiment. At the end of the semester, these marks are totalled and converted to a final practical mark out of 100, worth 25% of the course grade. During the semester, you can check your marks for the experiments completed via a link on the course web page.

Students who diligently attempt an experiment can expect to gain a passing score (12+). Students who show at least a minimum level of proficiency can expect a mark of 14, while high achievers will aim for 20 or higher.

Ten of the twenty five available marks are awarded for your answers to the pre-lab questions. To score full marks, you must answer all pre-lab questions correctly. You should be able to obtain (or deduce) the correct answers for pre-lab questions from the information provided in the notes (or references therein).

The experiments are a separate part of the course. Thus, while they may explore similar topics, students should not expect an exact overlap with the lecture material. Due to constraints of timetabling, students may encounter concepts prior to meeting them in lectures. The lab manual has been written to take this into account..

Option 2: Chemistry Undergraduate Research Experience (CURE)

n first year Chemistry, you are often given detailed instructions to enable you to complete an experiment in the three-hour time limit. However, real research usually does not come with detailed instructions. Existing procedures have to be modified to new situations and techniques need to be applied in new ways to answer novel questions. In CURE, you will undergo training in organic chemistry for three laboratory sessions (Weeks 3, 4 and 5 of semester) in a group of three, before the research phase for the final three weeks.

More details on this years CURE offering will be presented on the CHEM1200 Blackboard site. Numbers are restricted by the lab space we have available for this project, which means that you will have to apply for a place in the CURE laboratory group.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Pre-lab quizzes are submitted through Blackboard at least 30 minutes prior to coming to class. Laboratory results sheets for each experiment must be handed in for marking at the end of that laboratory session.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Workshop sessions are held every two weeks and are based on the material taught during the lectures. These sessions are open book so students may use lecture notes, textbooks and other course material/resources provided to them as they wish. Collaboration with other students during the session is encouraged.

Due to the large number of students enrolled, we the cohort is split into two groups every week there is a workshop scheduled: Wednesday group and Friday group

The assessed activities are to be completed during the designated session times and worksheets must be handed in before the student leaves. Attendance at the workshops is not compulsory, but highly recommended.

Workshop sessions held in weeks 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 are all assessed.

Your final mark for the in-class workshop assessment is an average of your 5 worksheets.

What should I do if I cannot attend a workshop session?

See the EXTENSION OR DEFERRAL AVAILABILITY NOTES below.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Each workshop sheet (hard copy) is submitted to the academic staff during the session.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The in-semester exam will test concepts from Module 1 only.

Note that student work in the laboratory is assessed within the laboratory sessions and is not examined on the in- or end of semester exams.

The date and further information for the in-semester examination will be notified through Bb, your SI-net account and email.

The exam will be face to face, invigilated examination held on the UQ St. Lucia Campus.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
The examination will consist of a mixture of multiple choice and short answer questions.

Examinable material for the end of semester examination include the lecture notes and lectures for all modules and relevant sections of the text book. Material from Module 1 will be necessary to make progress in Modules 2, 3 and 4, and some concepts of Module 1 applied in the conext of the concepts you learn in Modules 2, 3 and 4 be assessed in the end of semester exam. The main emphasis of the exam will however be on Modules 2 and 3.

To pass the course, you must score a weighted average of 40% or better on the combination of the in- and end of semester examinations i.e. if you fall below 40% on one of the exams, you must do proportionately better than 40% on the other exam.

The exam will be a face to face, invigilated examination held on UQ St. Lucia Campus.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Lecture series

Module 1: Chemical kinetics & acid/base chemistry.

Taught by Prof Lisbeth Grondahl (kinetics) and A/Prof Evan Moore (acids/bases)


Module 1 is examined in the in-semester examination.


Module 2: Transition metal chemistry

Taught by A/Prof Evan Moore


Module 3: Structure determination, organic stereochemistry and introduction to organic reactions

Taught by Dr Masnun Naher


Module 4: Organic reactions

Taught by Prof Craif Williams


Modules 2, 3 and 4 are examined in the end of semester examination.
